title: Financiers
subtitle: Small French almond cakes with a brown butter crust and moist, chewy crumb.
excerpt: Financiers are small French almond cakes made with brown butter, ground almonds, icing sugar, and egg whites. The batter comes together in 10 minutes with no mixer; the key technique is browning the butter until it smells of hazelnuts before adding it to the dry ingredients. The result is a dense, moist crumb with a slightly crisp outer shell. Makes 12 to 16 in a mini muffin tin.
sourceNotes: Financiers are attributed to a Parisian pastry chef, Lasne, who made them near the Paris Bourse in the 1890s to satisfy stockbrokers who wanted something small and neat that would not stain their suits. The rectangular gold-bar shape, which inspired the name, is traditional but any small mould works. The combination of ground almonds, icing sugar, brown butter (beurre noisette), and egg whites is the defining formula. The recipe has remained unchanged for over a century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a single layer once cool. Defrost at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: The batter doubles easily and keeps in the fridge for two days.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter keeps in the fridge for up to two days. Bake straight from the fridge.

recipeTools.notes: Or a traditional rectangular financier mould. Butter the tin well; the batter has no fat except for the butter mixed in.

glossaryTerms.definition: Brown butter. Butter cooked over medium heat until the milk solids toast and turn golden brown, giving a nutty, caramel smell and flavour. The key technique in financiers. Pull it from the heat as soon as you smell hazelnuts; a few more seconds and it burns.

content.text: . Butter goes into a small pan over medium heat and stays there, stirring occasionally, until it turns golden brown and smells of hazelnuts. It will foam before it colours; keep watching. Once brown, pour it immediately into the dry ingredients; it will carry on cooking in the pan if you leave it.
The batter benefits from a 30-minute rest in the fridge before baking. This is not essential but it gives a crisper outer shell.

items.prepNote: from large eggs, at room temperature

content.text: Combine {{almond-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}}, {{plain-flour}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Add {{egg-whites}} and {{vanilla-extract}} and stir to a thick paste.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} in a small saucepan over medium heat. Continue cooking, swirling the pan occasionally, until the butter foams, then subsides, and the milk solids at the bottom turn golden brown and smell of hazelnuts. This takes 4 to 6 minutes. Pour immediately into the batter and stir to combine.
Heat the oven to 200°C fan (220°C conventional). Butter the tin well. Spoon the batter into the holes, filling each to about two thirds.
Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until deep golden on top and a skewer comes out clean. Leave in the tin for 5 minutes, then turn onto a rack.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: The main risk is burning the butter.

items.fix: Brown butter goes from hazelnut to burnt in about 10 seconds. Watch it closely from the moment the foam subsides. If in doubt, pull it a shade early; the flavour develops as it cools.
Butter every surface including the sides. The batter is sticky. Run a thin knife around the edges before turning out.
